text: Up to six percent of patients harboring renal cell carcinoma have a neoplastic venous thrombus at diagnosis. Literature evidences suggest a beneficial impact of surgery in such category of patients. The surgical method selected depends on the level of tumor thrombus and the grade of occlusion of the inferior vena cava. In the last years, rapid assisted thrombectomy has been reported with promising perioperative results. To improve the tumor thrombus identification and management, some ancillary technologies have been introduced, like introvertive ultrasound.

Among the new imaging technologies developed to assist the surgeon during robotic procedures for renal cancer, three-D virtual models have been introduced, demonstrating a great impact in driving the decision-making process during the intervention, especially when superimposed over the real anatomy in an augmented reality setting. However, their role in robotic surgery for renal tumors with renal vein or vena cava thrombi has never been explored. The aim of the study is to explore the safety and feasibility of a three-D augmented reality guidance during robot-assisted radical nephrectomy for renal tumors with neoplastic thrombus in the renal vein and inferior vena cava.

The following video will show the case of a sixty-three years old female. After a single episode of gross hematuria, the ultrasound revealed a right renal tumor. CT scan confirmed the presence of a seventy millimeter right renal mass with a thirty millimeter tumor thrombus growing into the inferior vena cava. A robot assisted radical nephrectomy with robotic thrombectomy was planned. The preoperative imaging assessment was completed with a three-D virtual reconstruction to plan the surgical strategy and to drive the surgeon during the intervention. After isolation of the renal loge, the inferior vena cava was identified and dissected. Then, the main renal artery was identified and clipped with Hemolock, excluding the kidney from the blood flow.

The surgeon clips lumbar veins to manage the inferior vena cava safely. After dissecting the inferior vena cava and right renal vein, two vessel loops are placed. The three-D virtual model is overlapped onto the real anatomy using TilePro technology from the DaVinci Surgical Console, with a dedicated assistant performing hand-assisted overlapping using a three-D mouse. Augmented reality technology allows the surgeon to hide elements of the virtual model, enhancing the perception of the thrombus extension into the inferior vena cava.

The surgeon fine-tunes the vessel loops using virtual model guidance, interrupts the caval blood flow by tightening the loops and securing them with hemo-locks. A cavotomy is performed on the anterior face of the ostium with the renal vein, revealing the tumor thrombus beneath the venous wall. The thrombus is gently removed from the vena cava, and the caval wall is sutured with a continuous 4-0 suture secured with a lapra tie. The vessel loops are removed, restoring venous return, and the radical nephrectomy is completed.

Overall, five patients harboring renal cell carcinoma with tumor thrombi in the renal vein or inferior vena cava up to level one underwent a three-D virtual model augmented reality guided robot-assisted radical nephrectomy. Median age was seventy-four years old. Mean CT lesion size was seventy five point six millimeters. Tumor thrombus level was zero in three cases and one in two cases. Mean operative time was one hundred and twenty three minutes whilst inferior vena cava clamping time was nine point four minutes. Mean estimated blood loss was seven hundred and fifty milliliters. The mean length of stay was seven days. No intraoperative complications were recorded, whilst only two postoperative minor complications were found. Pathological examination confirmed clinical evidence. A clear cell carcinoma staged PT-IIIa and PT-IIIb was found in three and two cases respectively.

The overlay of virtual models over the operative field in real time allowed to perform an augmented reality surgery, increasing the surgeon's awareness of the tumor thrombus level. In conclusion, this study shows how the use of three-D virtual models, even in case of extreme surgeries such as for a robot-assisted radical nephrectomy with venous neoplastic thrombus, can allow to plan the most effective surgical strategy and to guide the surgeon during the procedure without risk of intraoperative complications.
